name: Soyuz-2
variant: 2.1b (payload, SSO/GTO capacity, and price figures below; flight totals are family-wide across 2.1a and 2.1b)

overview.value: Soyuz-2 is a family of Russian expendable medium-lift launch vehicles built by RKTs Progress, the seventh major iteration of the Soyuz rocket line. It flies mainly in two variants, the baseline 2.1a and the enhanced 2.1b, which adds a more powerful third-stage engine and, usually, a Fregat upper stage. The family has flown 165 times with 160 successes from Baikonur, Plesetsk, and Vostochny, launching crewed Soyuz spacecraft, Progress cargo craft, and a wide range of civil and military satellites. Its first crewed flight came on 9 April 2020, carrying Soyuz MS-16 to the International Space Station.

base.reason: established aggregator, single reference (Russian launch vehicle registry addition, 2026-07-13)

notes: Payload, GTO/SSO capacity, and price figures are for the Soyuz-2.1b variant, the higher-capacity of the two active configurations flown from Baikonur; flights_total and flights_successful cover the whole 2.1a plus 2.1b family per Wikipedia's combined infobox count. stages left out: sources state 3 or 4 depending on upper stage, not a single figure.

events.headline: Soyuz-2.1a launches its maiden suborbital test flight from Plesetsk
Soyuz-2.1b Fregat makes its first flight, launching the CoRoT satellite
Soyuz-2.1b fails carrying Meridian 2 after a third-stage fuel leak
events.cause: Bulging of the third-stage combustion chamber led to a fuel leak.

events.headline: Soyuz-2.1b explodes in flight, destroying the Meridian 5 satellite
events.cause: The third-stage engine shut down early and exploded.

events.headline: Soyuz-2.1a debuts at Vostochny Cosmodrome with Mikhailo Lomonosov
Soyuz-2.1a launches its first crewed mission, Soyuz MS-16, to the ISS
